coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots - driven epigenome reprogramming reshapes DNA methylation landscapes in rice

coredata.dc:description: DNA methylation plays a crucial role in the regulation of gene expression, ensuring normal growth and development and enabling responses to biotic and abiotic stresses in plants. Carbon dots (CDs) can participate in the entire plant life cycle and improve yield, however, the understanding of how CDs affect plant growth remains limited. Here, we demonstrate that CDs induce subtle but significant global DNA hypermethylation in rice, particularly in CHG and CHH contexts. This hypermethylation may be driven by the upregulation of OsCMTs triggered by CDs. CDs can stabilize i-motifs—potential regulators of active genes—in the promoter regions of OsCMT1 and OsCMT2, leading to increased CHG and CHH methylation. Our findings suggest that CDs modulate global DNA methylation changes, influencing the transcription of genes associated with phenotypic alterations in plants.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of CDs on rice phenotypic and physiological change
Impact of CDs on reprogramming of DNA methylation
Impact of CDs on differential DNA methylation in repetitive DNA sequences
Effect of CD-inducible DMRs on expression of genes with distinct biological relevance
Effect of CDs on differential expression of DNA methyltransferases

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BE2022425, BK20220028), National Key R&D Program of China (2024YFA1509300), National Natural Science Foundation of China (U23A20179, 32070561, 32201782, U20A2030, \u202C52272043, 52271223, 52472049), The Science and Technology Development Fund, Macau SAR (0009/2022/ITP), State Key Laboratory of Catalysis (2024SKL-A-014), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, the 111 Project, and Suzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nano & Soft Materials. We thank the Bioinformatics Center, Nanjing Agricultural University for providing computing facilities for data processing and analyses.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization and effects of CDs on seedling growth and photosynthetic activities in rice. (A) TEM image and the size distribution of CDs. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectrum and FT-IR spectrum of CDs. (C) The conductivity-titrant amount curve of CDs, a and b are the equivalence points of hydroxyl and carboxyl, respectively. (D) Effect of CDs on the growth of rice seedlings in the test tube. (E) Effect of different concentrations of CDs on the growth of sprout and root. (F) Comparison of the root and sprout length under light (left) and dark (right) conditions with or without CD treatment. (G) Comparison of PSII photochemical efficiency Fv/Fm in rice leaves with or without CD treatment. (H) Comparison of photochemical quenching (qP) in rice leaves with or without CD treatment. (I) Comparison of quantum yield (Y) PSI function in rice leaves with or without CD treatment. (J) Comparison of quantum yield (Y) of PSII function in rice leaves with or without CD treatment. (K) Comparison of electron transport (ETR) of PSI function in rice leaves with or without CD treatment. (L) Comparison of electron transport (ETR) of PSII function in rice leaves with or without CD treatment. Significance test was determined using t-test, ∗∗p < 0.001.

Characterization of BS-seq data sets. (A) A principal component analysis (PCA) showing the consistency between biologically replicated BS-seq data generated from the CD-treated sample or CK and the differences between the CD-treated sample and CK. (B) DNA methylation profiles showing the average methylation level of mCG, mCHG and mCHH context surrounding ± 2 kb from the transcription start sites (TSSs) to the transcription terminate sites (TTSs) of genes (left) or from the start to the end point of transposable elements (TEs) (Wilcoxon rank-sum test, “∗”: p < 0.05, “∗∗”: p < 0.01, “∗∗∗”: p < 0.001). (C) Boxplots showing DNA methylation levels of 7867 hyper- and 5390 hypo-methylated regions (DMRs) in the CD-treated sample relative to CK. Methylation levels of total C, CG, CHG, and CHH context of two biological replicates between the CD-treated sample and CK are shown (Wilcoxon rank-sum test, “∗∗∗∗”: p < 0.0001). (D) Genomic distributions of CD-induced hypo-DMRs and hyper-DMRs. The whole genome was partitioned into seven functionally annotated regions, including promoters (TSS up 1 kb), 5′ UTRs, Exons, Introns, 3′ UTRs, Downstream and distal intergenic regions.

Characterization of DNA methylation in CentO and TEs. (A) Southern blotting assay using genomic DNA, which was extracted from the CD-treated sample and CK for isoschizomers MspI and HapII treatment, probed with CentO. (B, C) DNA methylation profiles showing average methylation levels of CG, CHG and CHH context surrounding ± 2 kb from the start to the end point of class I and class II TEs (Wilcoxon rank-sum test, “∗”: p < 0.05, “∗∗”: p < 0.01).

Characterization of DMRs in association with DEGs. (A) Distributions of hyper DMRs (upper panel) and hypo DMRs (lower panel) around ± 2 kb of the TSSs or the TTSs of genes as compared to those of randomly selected genomic fragments of the same length. (B) GO term enrichment analyses of DMR-related DEGs related to CDs treatment.

Characterization of expression of DNA methyltransferase genes under CDs treatment. (A) qRT-PCR validation of DNA methyltransferase genes that maintain DNA methylation. (B) Circular dichorism (CD) spectra of selected PiMFSs oligoes in the promoter region of DNA methyltransferase genes (Student's t-test, “∗”: p < 0.05, “∗∗”: p < 0.01). (C) Enhancer validation of the i-motif located in the promoter of CMT2 gene with or without (Δ) putative i-motif formatting sequence (PiMFS) by using CDs mediated transient formation in wheat and rice leaves. Bar size = 50 μm.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots - driven epigenome reprogramming reshapes DNA methylation landscapes in rice

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: DNA methylation plays a crucial role in the regulation of gene expression, ensuring normal growth and development and enabling responses to biotic and abiotic stresses in plants. Carbon dots (CDs) can participate in the entire plant life cycle and improve yield, however, the understanding of how CDs affect plant growth remains limited. Here, we demonstrate that CDs induce subtle but significant global DNA hypermethylation in rice, particularly in CHG and CHH contexts. This hypermethylation may be driven by the upregulation of OsCMTs triggered by CDs. CDs can stabilize i-motifs—potential regulators of active genes—in the promoter regions of OsCMT1 and OsCMT2, leading to increased CHG and CHH methylation. Our findings suggest that CDs modulate global DNA methylation changes, influencing the transcription of genes associated with phenotypic alterations in plants.

CDs activate the expression of chromomethylase (CMT) genes by binding to i-motifs within these genes. This interaction induces genome-wide modifications in DNA methylation, which are associated with significant improvements in rice growth.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Rice is a staple food crop for a substantial proportion of the global population, making it essential to understand its adaptive mechanisms to diverse and changing environments to ensure stable yields and global food security [1,2]. DNA methylation, an epigenetic process involving the addition of a methyl group to a DNA base, plays a fundamental role in regulating various aspects of rice biology [3,4], including seed development [5], flowering time [6], and responses to biotic and abiotic stresses [7–12]. Recent advancements in rice DNA methylation research have significantly enhanced our understanding of its functional roles and implications for rice improvement4. Studies have elucidated the distribution and function of different methylation types (CG, CHG, and CHH) in the rice genome and the roles of methylation enzymes such as OsCMT3a and OsDRM2 in gene expression regulation and transposable element silencing [8,13,14]. Genome-wide methylation levels in rice have been found to be significantly higher than those in Arabidopsis, with distinct patterns in gene transcription termination regions compared to promoters [13,15]. Additionally, research has shown dynamic methylation remodeling during male gametogenesis, highlighting the functional significance of methylation in rice reproduction. In response to environmental stresses, DNA methylation has been identified as a crucial mechanism for rice to adapt to drought, salt, and temperature stresses [16]. Investigating DNA methylation in rice aids in elucidating the mechanisms of gene expression regulation, genome stability maintenance, and stress response, thereby providing a theoretical basis and technical support for rice genetic improvement and stress-resistant breeding.

Carbon dots (CDs) are considered as an environmentally friendly nanomaterial and are increasingly being applied in plant systems [17–20]. CDs can participate in the entire plant life cycle and significantly improve yield, the biological activity of CDs is closely linked to their surface functional groups. Functional moieties such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups enhance the biocompatibility and bioavailability of CDs, which may facilitate nutrient uptake and intracellular signaling, ultimately promoting plant growth and photosynthesis [17,18]. Pioneering studies have demonstrated the penetration of CDs into various plants and their beneficial effects on plant growth, including: (i) promoting seed germination and enhancing root elongation; (ii) increasing the absorption and delivery of ionic nutrients; (iii) enhancing enzyme activity; (iv) increasing resistance to plant diseases; and (v) degrading into plant hormone analogues and CO2. Preliminary results suggest that CDs can reach the rice cell nucleus, elevating the expression levels of the thionine gene (Os06g32600), thereby increasing disease resistance [17,18,21]. Additionally, CDs can efficiently deliver plasmid DNA for expression in wheat and rice, or deliver siRNA into the leaves of Nicotiana benthamiana and tomato for gene silencing [19,20,22–24].

Although numerous studies have demonstrated the physiological and biochemical effects of carbon-based nanomaterials on plants, their potential to induce long-term, heritable changes through epigenetic regulation remains largely unexplored. DNA methylation plays a crucial role in plant development and stress adaptation by regulating gene expression without altering DNA sequence. Given the small size, high surface activity, and bioavailability of CDs, we hypothesize that CDs may influence the plant epigenome, thereby reshaping growth-related gene expression.

Here, we reported the bioeffects of CDs for reprogramming of DNA methylation and epigenetic modulation in rice. We conducted bisulfite-sequencing (BS-seq) to uncover how CDs induce changes in DNA methylation in rice. We demonstrate that CDs induce subtle but significant global DNA hypermethylation in rice, particularly in CHG and CHH contexts. This hypermethylation is likely driven by the upregulation of OsCMTs (CHROMOMETHYLTRANSFERASEs) triggered by CDs. Intriguingly, CDs were found to stabilize i-motifs—potential regulators of active genes—in the promoter regions of OsCMT1 and OsCMT2, leading to increased CHG and CHH methylation. It reveals for the first time the effects of CDs on epigenomic modifications in rice from a genome-wide perspective.

CDs were synthesized by using an electrochemical etching method as previously described with minor modifications [18]. Two graphite rods (99.99 %, Alfa Aesar Co. Ltd.) were used as anode and cathode in ultrapure water. The static potential of 30 V was applied to the two electrodes for 3 weeks under continuously intense magnetic stirring. The resulting lectrolyte was filtered through slow-speed quantitative filter paper. The resulting filtrate was centrifuged at 18,000 rpm for 30 min to remove the large precipitated graphite oxide and graphite particles. The supernatant was the water-soluble CDs solution (∼5 nm) for use in the study. The concentration of the solution was estimated by freeze-drying 100 ml of CDs solution and weighing the resulting dry powder.

CDs were applied as a foliar spray to rice seedlings. Specifically, one hundred germinated rice seeds were sown in 9 cm diameter Petri dishes. After three days of growth, the seedlings were treated every three days with 5 mL of CD solution at a concentration of 0.56 mg/mL using a hand-held atomizer, while water was used as the control. This treatment continued for three weeks, after which leaf tissues were collected for DNA extraction.

100 mg fresh leaves of WT without (CK) or with CD treatment were used for total RNA extraction using TRIzol reagent (Invitrogen). After treating with DNase for removal of genomic DNA contamination, total 3 μg of the purified RNA was reversely transcribed into first-stranded cDNAs (SuperScript IV, Thermo Fisher). Quantitative real-time PCR (qRT-PCR) was performed in an ABI StepOnePlus instrument with the SYBR green detection system (Invitrogen). PCR programs were as below: 40 cycles with 10 s at 95 °C and 30 s at 60 °C per cycle. Biological triplicates for each sample were performed for RT-qPCR assay. All results were normalized relative to the expression level of the housekeeping gene beta-actin. qRT-PCR results are shown as a relative expression level calculated using the 2-ΔΔCT method. P values were calculated with Student's t-test. The primers used in this assay are listed in Supplemental Table S1.

TEs were annotated by running RepeatMasker (version open-4.0.9) against the rice reference genome sequence (MSU version 7.0) with the repeat library from RepBase (v20181026). To detect methylation differences of TEs between CD-treated seedlings and CK, we compared the methylation levels for each methylation context in 12 major orders belonging to two TE classes: Class I (retrotransposons) and Class II (DNA transposons) (Supplemental Table S2).

500 μl of 5 μM i-motif oligonucleotide as mentioned in the text were reconstructed in i-motif stabilization buffer (50 mM Tris-AcOH, pH = 5.5) followed by with or without CD treatments. The reconstructed solution with or without CD treatments was measured in an optical chamber (1 mm path length) with a JASCO J-815 spectropolarimeter (Tokyo, Japan). A deoxygenation atmosphere was maintained by using dry purified nitrogen gas. The net CD signal was obtained after subtracting the solution background.
The BS-seq data described in this study have been deposited in the NCBI Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO; http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/geo/) under accession number GSE189428.

In our work, CDs were synthesized using an electrochemical etching method conducted at room temperature according to our previous reports. A typical transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the as-synthesized CDs is shown in Fig. 1 A. The CDs are well-dispersed and uniform with the diameters of approximately 2–7 nm. The size distribution (inset in Fig. 1A) was calculated by measuring approximately 200 particles. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CDs (Fig. 1B, red trace) shows a characteristic peak at 231 nm, attributing to the π-π∗ transition of CC bonds. While the FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 1B, black trace) was used to analyze the surface functional groups of CDs, identifying a peak at 3490 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of O–H groups. Additional peaks are observed at 1629 cm−1 and 1385 cm−1, associating with the stretching vibrations of CO/CC and C–O bonds, respectively. These hydrophilic functional groups on the surfaces of CDs, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, endow the CDs with excellent water solubility, making them highly suitable for various biological applications. To quantify the hydroxyl and carboxylic acid on the surface of the water-soluble CDs, base titration was performed to measure the total quantity of both hydroxyls and carboxylic acid following a standard method. Subsequently, the relative content of hydroxyl vs. carboxylic acid of the water-soluble CDs (about 3.3:1) was determined by conductometric titration shown in Fig. 1C. The Raman spectrum of CDs is shown in Fig. S1, where displays the characteristic D and G bands. The stability of CDs were also measured under different temperatures, pH values and in PB aqueous solution, which is shown in Fig. S2. The above results are consistent with our previous reports [18,25]. During this experiment, the CDs we re-synthesized are consistent with those previously reported in our papers in terms of basic properties such as morphology, size and size distribution, surface functional group distribution, and the ratio of surface hydroxyl to carboxyl groups. Therefore, the obtained CDs possess the same performance as the previous CDs, so ensuring the inheritance and continuity of this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CDs on rice phenotypic and physiological change
ce:section.ce:para: To assess effect of CDs on phenotypic and physiological changes in rice, we transferred 7-day-old seedlings into liquid culture medium containing 0.56 mg/ml CDs and cultured for additional 10 days. As a result, we observed that CD-treated seedlings grew faster than CK (Fig. 1D). Moreover, CDs exhibited concentration-dependent effect on growth of roots and sprouts (Fig. 1E). As shown in Fig. 1F, the average length of CD-treated roots and sprouts was increased by 50 % and 86 % under light conditions, and increased by 98 % and 40 % under dark conditions. we further measured photosynthetic efficiency of photosystem I (PSI) and photosystem II (PSII) with distinct architectures and functions during photosynthesis. CDs have been reported to enhance photosynthesis of dicotyledons 17. We found that CDs did not affect photochemical efficiency (Fv/Fm) (Fig. 1G), while increased photochemical quenching (qP) (Fig. 1H). Furthermore, CDs increased functions of PSII instead of PSI, such as an increase of the quantum yield (Y) and relative rate of electron transport (ETR) of PSII (Fig. 1I–L). Our results show that CDs specifically boost PSII activity rather than PSI, which may be due to several reasons. PSII is key to light harvesting and water splitting—processes that are sensitive to reactive oxygen species (ROS) possibly generated by CDs. These ROS may enhance PSII's efficiency by supporting electron transport. Also, surface groups on CDs like hydroxyl and carboxyl may interact with thylakoid membrane components to favor PSII. Additionally, CDs might act as light-harvesting antennas, improving light absorption for PSII. Altogether, CDs likely improve photosynthesis by selectively enhancing PSII function in rice.

Collectively, the results above demonstrate that CDs can induce both phenotypic and physiological changes in rice. In a previous study, we reported that treatment with the same carbon dots increased main root length and Rubisco activity in rice and maize [18,26]. Similarly, in another study on Arabidopsis thaliana, we observed a 42 % increase in Rubisco activity following carbon dot treatment [25]. These findings, viewed from a different perspective, suggest that the CDs used in this study share the same properties as those reported earlier, exhibiting conserved functions across different plant species, including rice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of CDs on reprogramming of DNA methylation
ce:section.ce:para: To address genome-wide effects of CDs on DNA methylation dynamics in rice, we generated highly correlated biological replicates for bisulfite sequencing (BS-seq), as confirmed by principal component analysis (PCA) (Fig. 2 A; Supplemental Fig. S3A). The methylation levels of total cytosines (C), CG, CHG, and CHH (H = A, C, or T) were 21.23 %, 72.5 %, 38.17 %, and 6.39 %, respectively, in CD-treated samples, compared to 20.77 %, 71.96 %, 37.2 %, and 5.89 % in controls (CK) (Supplemental Fig. S3B). The relative proportions of methylated cytosines for CG, CHG, and CHH contexts were 52.96 %, 26 %, and 21.04 % in CD-treated samples, compared to 53.8 %, 26.43 %, and 19.76 % in controls (Supplemental Fig. S3C).

To further investigate methylation dynamics, we plotted the average DNA methylation levels across ±2 kb from transcription start sites (TSSs) to transcription termination sites (TTSs) for all genes, as well as across transposable elements (TEs). In CD-treated samples, gene body regions exhibited higher CG, CHG, and CHH methylation levels compared to controls. For TEs, CG and CHG methylation levels were elevated upstream of TSSs and downstream of TTSs, while CHH methylation was higher across all regions analyzed (Fig. 2B).

To enhance the statistical power of identifying differentially methylated sites (DMSs) and regions (DMRs), we combined the two biological replicates for analysis. This approach yielded a total of 153,770 DMSs and 13,257 DMRs in the CD-treated samples compared to controls (CK) (Supplemental Table S1). Consistent DNA methylation levels, including hypo- and hyper-DMRs across all cytosine contexts, were observed in each replicate within the respective samples (Fig. 2C).

To assess the genomic distribution of DMRs, the genome was partitioned into seven functionally annotated regions: 1 kb upstream of transcription start sites (TSSs; putative promoters), introns, exons, downstream of transcription termination sites (TTSs), 5′ UTRs, 3′ UTRs, and distal intergenic regions. Hypomethylated CG, CHG, and CHH DMRs were predominantly located in introns, downstream of TTSs, and exons compared to their hypermethylated counterparts (Fig. 2D).
Taken together, the above analyses demonstrate that CD treatment induces subtle yet significant DNA hypermethylation in rice, with a pronounced global increase in CHH methylation levels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of CDs on differential DNA methylation in repetitive DNA sequences
ce:section.ce:para: Highly repetitive DNA sequences in the rice genome, including centromeric tandem repeats (CentO), Class I (retrotransposon) and Class II (DNA transposon) TEs, are usually highly methylated [27]. To examine DNA methylation changes within rice repetitive DNA sequences under CD treatment, we trimmed the same amount of genomic DNA as indicated using isoschizomers, MspI and HpaII, followed by southern blotting assay using CentO as a probe. Both enzymes can cut the same CCGG sequences but with a distinct sensitivity to the internal C methylation [28]. We observed that the signal intensity of ladders at a lower level from the HpaII-trimmed sample was stronger in CK than the corresponding one in the CD treated sample (Fig. 3 A). Thus, this result showed that CD treatment induced an elevation of DNA methylation with internal C methylation of CCGG sequences within CentO, thereby suppressing HpaII digestion.

We next examined DNA methylation changes in specific Class I and Class II transposable elements (TEs). Overall, both classes exhibited more hypermethylated DMRs than hypomethylated DMRs (Supplemental Fig. S4A). Notably, hyper- and hypomethylated DMRs were highly enriched in certain TE subfamilies, including LTR-other, SINE, MULE-MuDR, and hAT, further confirming CDs-induced DNA hypermethylation within TEs (Supplemental Fig. S4B).

To investigate these changes in detail, we plotted DNA methylation levels in CG, CHG, and CHH contexts across ±2 kb flanking the start and end points of individual Class I and II TEs. Compared to CK, the upstream and downstream regions of most TEs showed increased methylation in all three contexts (CG, CHG, and CHH) in the CD-treated samples (Fig. 3B and 3C). Exceptions were observed in specific regions: LTR_Copia showed higher CHG methylation, while LINE, LTR_Copia, LTR_Gypsy, SINE, hAT, Helitron, and MULE_MuDR regions exhibited elevated CHH methylation levels in the CDs-treated samples. Collectively, these results demonstrate that TE subfamilies exhibit differential DNA methylation patterns in response to CD treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CD-inducible DMRs on expression of genes with distinct biological relevance
ce:section.ce:para: To assess how DMRs are involved in differential gene expression during the CD treatment, we plotted normalized hypo or hyper DMR density across ±2 kb of the TSSs or the TTSs of genes. We found that hyper or hypo DMRs were significantly enriched at the upstream of TSSs or the downstream of TTSs, but less enriched in gene body as compared to random control (Fig. 4 A).

After associating DMRs with DEGs, we found that hyper and hypo DMRs were corresponded to 21 down-regulated and 21 up-regulated genes, respectively, reflecting a negative association between DNA methylation and expression of overlapping genes. However, we found that hyper and hypo CHH were corresponded to up-regulation of 25 genes and down-regulation of 23 genes, respectively (Supplemental Table S4), indicative of a positive association between CHH methylation and expression of overlapping genes. To examine if those DEGs have any biological relevance, we conducted GO terms enrichment analyses. We found that hypo CHH related down-regulated genes were mainly enriched in functions related to photosynthesis and thylakoid, while hyper CHH related up-regulated genes were main enriched in response to biotic stimuli and extracellular regions, by contrast, hypo DMR-related up-regulated genes were overrepresented in GO terms associated with extracellular region, apoplast, nutrient reservoir activity and manganese ion binding (Fig. 4B).

Genome-wide DNA methylation plays a crucial role in plant growth, development, and stress resistance. Our study represents the first report of CDs influencing DNA methylation in rice. The results not only highlight the potential of CDs to modulate DNA methylation patterns but also provide valuable insights into the mechanisms of epigenetic regulation in rice. Given the prominent increase in CHH methylation levels, this discovery could have far-reaching implications for understanding how nanomaterials like CDs interact with plant genomes and affect their epigenetic landscape. Moreover, this finding could inform future strategies in crop improvement, enhancing the resilience and adaptability of rice to environmental stress. Overall, our study adds a new dimension to the field of rice epigenetics, offering new opportunities for manipulating DNA methylation for agricultural advancements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs on differential expression of DNA methyltransferases
ce:section.ce:para: In plants, DNA methylation in CG, CHG, and CHH contexts is regulated by three types of DNA methyltransferases: MET1, CMT, and DRM, respectively [29,30]. To determine whether the subtle but significant global elevation in DNA methylation observed during CDs exposure is associated with the upregulation of DNA methyltransferases, we analyzed the TPM values of all annotated DNA methyltransferases in the rice genome. While a general trend of up-regulation was observed, the differences did not reach statistical significance (Supplemental Table S5).

To validate this trend, we performed RT-qPCR for selected genes and found that OsCMT1, OsCMT2, and OsCMT3 were significantly up-regulated in CD-treated samples compared to controls (CK) (Fig. 5 A). OsCMT1, which is mainly involved in CHG methylation, likely contributes to the observed increase in CHG methylation, particularly in transposable elements. OsCMT2 plays a crucial role in CHH methylation, and its upregulation may contribute to the observed changes in gene expression related to growth and stress resistance. Although OsCMT3 is responsible for CG methylation, its role in this study appears to be less pronounced compared to the other two genes, suggesting that CHG and CHH methylation pathways are more heavily influenced by CDs in this context. These findings suggest that CDs treatment induces the up-regulation of OsCMTs, contributing to the observed increases in CHG and CHH methylation levels.

It has been reported that nanomaterials can stabilize human telomeric i-motif formation even under physiological or alkaline conditions [31,32]. Moreover, stabilization of i-motif by the binding ligands can activate gene expression [33,34]. We then investigated whether CDs can stabilize i-motif formation from putative i-motif forming sequences (PiMFSs) in the promoters of OsCMTs, thereby facilitating their expression. We conducted circular dichroism (CD) assays using synthesized oligonucleotides of PiMFSs from the promoter regions of six DNA methyltransferases (Supplemental Table S6). The results showed that CDs treatment stabilized i-motif formation for OsCMT1, OsCMT2, and OsDRM2. However, for OsDNMT2, OsMTE1-2, and OsCMT3, the i-motif absorbance peak amplitude was lower and shifted slightly to the left, indicating instability (Fig. 5B, Supplemental Fig. S5). These findings suggest that CDs preferentially stabilize the i-motif formation of specific DNA methyltransferases, contributing to cytosine context-related DNA methylation changes.

To evaluate whether PiMFS sequences in these promoters’ function as enhancers to activate gene expression, we performed enhancer validation using CDs-mediated transient transformations in wheat and rice leaves. The results confirmed that intact i-motifs enhanced the expression of the reporter gene GFP, whereas mutated i-motifs lacking PiMFS sequences failed to do so, validating that PiMFS sequences act as enhancers to modulate gene expression under CDs treatment (Fig. 5C). Taken together, these results demonstrate that the effects of CDs on differential gene expression are partially mediated by CMT2 and other DNA methyltransferases.

In this study, we demonstrate for the first time that carbon dots (CDs) can modulate epigenetic regulation in rice by stabilizing i-motif structures in the promoter regions of DNA methyltransferase genes. This interaction leads to the upregulation of OsCMT1-3 and a genome-wide increase in CHG and CHH methylation levels, particularly in gene and transposable element regions. Our bisulfite sequencing results provide clear evidence that CDs act as epigenetic modulators, potentially influencing genome stability and gene expression. These findings not only advance our understanding of nano–bio interactions in plants but also suggest new strategies for improving crop performance through materials-based interventions.

Ranran Huang: Investigation. Hui Huang: Methodology. Shentong Tao: Formal analysis. Zhenyu Luo: Data curation. Yujie Zhao: Investigation. Linwei She: Investigation. Jinjing Yang: Investigation. Yingjie Lei: Data curation. Qianfeng Li: Formal analysis. Yang Liu: Project administration. Zhenhui Kang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Wenli Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Jian Huang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
The authors declare no competing interests.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the Bioinformatics Center, Nanjing Agricultural University for providing computing facilities for data processing and analyses.

This work is supported by Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BE2022425, BK20220028), National Key R&D Program of China (2024YFA1509300), National Natural Science Foundation of China (U23A20179, 32070561, 32201782, U20A2030, ‬52272043, 52271223, 52472049), The Science and Technology Development Fund, Macau SAR (0009/2022/ITP), State Key Laboratory of Catalysis (2024SKL-A-014), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, the 111 Project, and Suzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nano & Soft Materials. We thank the Bioinformatics Center, Nanjing Agricultural University for providing computing facilities for data processing and analyses.
